description: Eval suite for game-dev skill ecosystem core skills

evals.prompt: Create a database schema for a real-time card game with players, decks, cards, matches, and match history
evals.expected_output: Drizzle ORM schema using pgTable with: players table (id, username, email, stats JSONB, timestamps, soft delete), item_definitions for card definitions (name, type enum, rarity enum, baseStats JSONB for flexible card properties, metadata JSONB), inventory table for player-owned cards (playerId FK, itemDefinitionId FK, properties JSONB for card-specific state), a sessions/matches table (playerId FK, gameMode, state JSONB for match state, startedAt, endedAt), events table for match history (playerId FK, type, data JSONB). Must include: indexes on playerId and type columns, composite indexes for common queries like player's cards filtered by type, JSONB for flexible card properties instead of hardcoded columns, UUID primary keys via crypto.randomUUID(), timestamps on all tables, soft deletes with deletedAt.

evals.prompt: I need to add a leaderboard to my game that shows weekly and all-time rankings
evals.expected_output: Uses the existing leaderboards table pattern with: playerId FK, category text, score real, rank integer, period using leaderboardPeriodEnum ('daily','weekly','monthly','seasonal','alltime'). Must include: unique composite index on (playerId, category, period) to prevent duplicates, composite index on (category, period, score) for efficient ranked queries, a window function query using ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category, period ORDER BY score DESC) for computing ranks, separate rows per period (weekly + alltime) rather than computed views, updatedAt timestamp for cache invalidation.

evals.prompt: Design inventory system for an idle game where players collect resources and craft items
evals.expected_output: Uses generic item_definitions table with itemTypeEnum for different resource/item types, rarity enum, baseStats JSONB for resource properties, stackable boolean with maxStack. Inventory table with playerId FK, itemDefinitionId FK, quantity for stackable resources, properties JSONB for instance-specific state. Currencies table with playerId FK, currencyType text, amount integer, lifetimeEarned for tracking. Must use entity system pattern — no hardcoded resource columns, all extensible via JSONB metadata. Composite index on (playerId, itemDefinitionId) for efficient lookups. Events table for transaction history logging crafting and resource changes.

evals.prompt: Add a new quest: 'The Merchant's Secret' — player must steal a letter from the royal archives for a suspicious merchant
evals.expected_output: Agent reads quest-narrative-coherence SKILL.md FIRST before any content creation. Runs the 5-step coherence check: (1) loads world-lore.md to understand existing factions, locations, and political structure, (2) checks quest-registry.md for existing quests involving the merchant, royal archives, or theft themes to avoid contradictions, (3) validates the merchant character against established NPCs and faction alignments, (4) confirms the royal archives exist as a location in the world, (5) checks that the quest rewards and difficulty fit the progression curve. Only after all 5 checks pass does content creation begin. Output includes coherence report documenting each check result.

evals.prompt: Create a character: General Mira, head of the city guard, who secretly works for the thieves guild
evals.expected_output: Agent reads quest-narrative-coherence SKILL.md FIRST before creating the character. Runs coherence checks: (1) loads world-lore.md to check existing city guard leadership and thieves guild membership, (2) validates that no existing character already holds the 'head of city guard' position, (3) checks faction relationships — city guard and thieves guild must not be in a state that makes dual membership impossible, (4) reviews existing quests in quest-registry.md for any that reference the city guard leadership or thieves guild hierarchy that would contradict, (5) validates temporal consistency — the character's backstory must fit the world timeline. Flags any contradictions found before proceeding with character creation.

evals.prompt: Set up a WebSocket game server that handles 100 concurrent players in separate rooms
evals.expected_output: Elysia WebSocket server using Bun runtime with: typed message protocol (MessageType enum, ServerMessage/ClientMessage discriminated unions), RoomManager class that creates/joins/leaves rooms with player capacity limits, player connection lifecycle (onOpen registers player, onClose cleans up room membership, heartbeat timeout detection), server-authoritative message handling where client sends intentions and server validates before broadcasting. Room pattern with Map<roomId, Room> where Room tracks connected players, game state, and tick loop. WebSocket upgrade handler with authentication. Must NOT trust client state — all game logic runs server-side.

evals.prompt: Add a game loop to my server that runs at 20 ticks per second and broadcasts state to all players
evals.expected_output: Fixed-timestep game loop at 20Hz (50ms per tick) using setInterval or Bun timer. Delta time computation tracking previous tick timestamp and computing dt = (now - lastTick) / 1000 for frame-independent simulation. Each tick: process input queue, update game state with dt, generate state snapshot, broadcast to all connected players in the room via WebSocket. State snapshot should be minimal — only changed entities or use delta compression. Broadcast uses room pattern to send only to players in the same room. Includes tick budget monitoring — log warning if tick processing exceeds 50ms budget. Loop cleanup on room empty or server shutdown.

evals.prompt: I need a Redis sorted set for a global leaderboard with 1 million players. Show me the commands, data structure, and how to page through the top 100.
evals.expected_output: Uses Redis ZADD with player ID as member and score as the sorted set value. Key naming convention like leaderboard:global:alltime. ZADD command with NX or GT flag to only update when score improves. ZREVRANGE or ZREVRANGEBYSCORE for top-N queries with WITHSCORES. ZREVRANK for fetching a specific player's rank in O(log N). Pagination via ZREVRANGE leaderboard:global:alltime 0 99 for first page, 100 199 for second. Covers TTL strategy for time-windowed leaderboards (weekly resets via EXPIREAT at Sunday midnight). Notes memory implications at 1M members and recommends monitoring with MEMORY USAGE.

evals.prompt: Players in my game have ephemeral session state (current room, health, position) that needs to be read on every tick. Design the Redis key schema and expiry strategy.
evals.expected_output: Key schema like session:{playerId} as a Redis Hash with fields for roomId, health, posX, posY, lastSeen. HSET/HGET/HMGET for efficient partial reads without fetching full object. EXPIRE set to session timeout (e.g., 30s) refreshed on each client heartbeat via EXPIRE session:{playerId} 30. On disconnect, cleanup via keyspace notification or explicit DEL. Covers HSCAN for iterating all sessions in a room. Notes that position/health are ephemeral — only persist to Neon (PostgreSQL) on graceful logout or periodic checkpoint, not on every tick. Recommends Redis pipeline for batching multiple HSET calls per tick to reduce round-trips.

evals.prompt: My matchmaking queue has 500 players waiting. Design an expanding search radius algorithm that finds fair matches without making players wait too long.
evals.expected_output: Expanding bracket algorithm: start with tight skill range (e.g., ±50 MMR), expand by fixed increment (±25 MMR) every N seconds of wait time. Priority queue ordered by wait time — longest-waiting players get matched first within the expanded bracket. Max expansion cap to prevent wildly unfair matches (e.g., ±300 MMR hard ceiling). Tracks waitTime per player and recomputes acceptable range as a function of wait duration. BullMQ job per queued player with exponential backoff on the bracket expansion. Separate queues per game mode. On match found: atomically dequeue both players, create match record, notify via WebSocket. Includes metrics: avg wait time, match quality score (MMR delta of matched players).

evals.prompt: Implement an Elo rating update after a 1v1 match. Show the calculation and how to persist the new ratings atomically.
evals.expected_output: Standard Elo formula: expected score E_a = 1 / (1 + 10^((R_b - R_a) / 400)), new rating R_a' = R_a + K * (S_a - E_a) where K is K-factor (32 for new players, 16 for established). K-factor tiers based on games played or current rating. Drizzle transaction that atomically: reads both player ratings, computes new values, updates players table, inserts match_results record with pre/post ratings for audit trail. Handles draws (S=0.5) and disconnects (treated as loss for disconnecting player). Includes rating floor (minimum 100) and provisional period logic for new players. Notes: never update rating outside a database transaction to avoid race conditions from concurrent matches.

evals.prompt: Track when players stop logging in after a major patch. What events should I emit, how do I store them, and how do I compute D1, D7, and D30 retention?
evals.expected_output: Emit player_login event with playerId, timestamp, platform, appVersion, sessionId. Store in events table (Drizzle schema) with JSONB data column. Retention cohort query: cohort is players who first logged in on date D (first_login_date), retention on day N counts players who also logged in on date D+N. SQL using window functions: first identify each player's first login date, then LEFT JOIN login events for D+1, D+7, D+30, compute ratio. Separate the patch cohort by filtering first_login_date >= patch_release_date. Notes that D7 post-patch drop indicates content issues vs D1 drop indicating onboarding/performance issues. Recommends daily materialized view refresh for retention dashboards rather than live queries at scale.

evals.prompt: I want to track a purchase funnel: player sees shop → opens item → clicks buy → completes purchase. How do I instrument this and compute drop-off rates?
evals.expected_output: Emit four events: shop_viewed, item_detail_opened (with itemId, itemType), purchase_initiated (with itemId, price, currency), purchase_completed (with itemId, price, currency, transactionId). All events include sessionId for funnel stitching. Funnel query using CTEs: count distinct sessionIds that triggered each event, compute conversion rate between each step as percentage. SQL pattern: shop_opens CTE, then LEFT JOIN item_opens ON sessionId, then LEFT JOIN purchase_starts, then LEFT JOIN purchase_completes. Drop-off rate = 1 - (next_step_count / current_step_count). Segment by item category, player level, or platform to identify where specific player segments drop. Recommends sessionId-based funnel (not playerId) to correctly handle players who see the shop multiple times.

evals.prompt: Design a biome system where dungeon room types have weighted random selection — desert rooms 40%, cave 35%, ruins 25%. Show the implementation and how to adjust weights per floor depth.
evals.expected_output: Weighted random selection using a cumulative weight array: build prefix sums [40, 75, 100], generate random number 0–100, use binary search to find the corresponding biome. Biome definition as a typed config object with weight, roomTypes array, enemySpawnTable, and lootTable references. Floor depth modifier: multiply base weights by a depth scalar (e.g., ruins weight increases +5% per floor, desert decreases) then renormalize to 100%. Implementation in TypeScript with a selectBiome(depth: number): BiomeType function. Server-side RNG using a seeded PRNG (e.g., mulberry32) so dungeon layout is reproducible from a seed — client can request the same layout without re-rolling. Notes genre-agnostic constraint: biome names are data, not hardcoded — config-driven so new biomes require no code changes.

evals.prompt: My free currency (gems) is inflating because players earn too many from daily quests. What sinks should I add and how do I measure the inflation rate with SQL?
evals.expected_output: Sink recommendations: premium cosmetics purchasable only with gems, stamina/energy refills for time-gated systems, crafting accelerators, limited-time auction bids, battle pass tiers. Each sink should have a clear value proposition so players choose to spend. Inflation rate query: SELECT date_trunc('day', createdAt) as day, SUM(CASE WHEN type = 'earn' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) as earned, SUM(CASE WHEN type = 'spend' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) as spent, SUM(SUM(CASE WHEN type = 'earn' THEN amount ELSE -amount END)) OVER (ORDER BY day) as circulating_supply FROM currency_events GROUP BY day. Rising circulating_supply indicates inflation. Target earn/spend ratio close to 1.0 per week. Recommends currency velocity metric (total spend / circulating supply per period) and alerts when velocity drops below threshold indicating hoarding behavior.

evals.prompt: I'm building a sci-fi colony game. Help me design two factions — a corporate mining conglomerate and a native resistance movement — with interlocking goals, tensions, and a reason for players to care about both sides.
evals.expected_output: Must use the faction-bible template structure for each faction, covering: public goals vs hidden goals, territory, resources controlled, enemies, allies, and gameplay role. The corporate faction needs concrete wants (resource extraction quotas, shareholder returns) and a hidden agenda (covering up ecological damage or prior contact evidence). The resistance needs clear motivation beyond 'they're oppressed' — specific historical event (disaster, broken treaty, land seizure) that created the conflict. Geography shapes both factions: resource nodes the corporation controls must be culturally or ecologically significant to the resistance. Must include at least one interlocking relationship where both factions want the same thing for opposite reasons, creating player-choice tension. Covers the principle that history creates present tension — the current conflict must trace to a specific past event. Must address tone consistency and note what fits/doesn't fit the sci-fi colony setting.

evals.prompt: My fantasy RPG has a magic system but players keep finding loopholes that break the game. How do I design a magic system with clear rules, costs, and limits that prevents power creep while keeping it interesting?
evals.expected_output: Must apply the 'rules before exceptions' principle: establish what is possible and impossible before any exceptions exist. Magic system design must include: a clear cost mechanism (stamina, health, resources, time) that makes every spell a meaningful decision, hard limits that cannot be bypassed (rule out specific interaction categories that would be broken), and a consistent internal logic players can intuit without reading documentation (Will Wright's simulation principle). Must distinguish between soft limits (designed to be pushed against) and hard limits (absolute). Covers the principle that magic/tech systems need clear costs and limits as a prerequisite to any special cases. Must address 'show, don't exposition' — the magic system's rules should be demonstrable through environmental examples and NPC behavior, not a rulebook. Includes guidance on documenting the magic system in the world bible so all content creators respect the same constraints.

evals.prompt: My RPG has three different faction allegiances the player can choose. How do I structure the main story so all three paths feel intentional and reach satisfying endings without tripling my writing workload?
evals.expected_output: Must use the branching narrative model with convergence points — not three fully separate stories. Structure: shared Act 1 (same hook, inciting incident, and world introduction regardless of faction choice), diverging Act 2 paths that share key plot beats at different locations/with different characters (the same 'midpoint twist' hits all three paths but through the lens of each faction), convergence point before Act 3 where all paths lead to the same final conflict with faction-specific framing. Must cover the three-act structure adapted for games with approximate percentages: setup 10-20%, confrontation 60-70%, resolution 10-20%. Each ending must feel earned by the player's journey — no 'true ending' or 'bad ending' framing, each reflects the faction's values. Must address the 'illusion of choice' anti-pattern — faction choice must produce visibly different outcomes in Act 2 or players will feel cheated. Covers consequence visibility: players must see the impact of their faction allegiance through world state changes, NPC reactions, and available missions.

evals.prompt: I want to tell my game's backstory without info-dump cutscenes. How do I use environmental storytelling and layered narrative so casual players get the story and dedicated players find deeper lore?
evals.expected_output: Must apply Miyazaki's layered narrative approach: broad strokes accessible to all players (main story beats conveyed through gameplay and major NPC interactions), mid-layer for engaged players (optional dialogue, visible environmental details, accessible collectibles), deep layer for dedicated players (item descriptions, hidden notes, obscure environmental details that recontextualize the surface story). Environmental storytelling techniques: architecture and decay patterns show time passage and past events, enemy placement and behavior implies territorial history, item descriptions reveal faction relationships and historical events. Must address the 'exposition dump' anti-pattern — characters should live in the world, not explain it. Covers the principle that 'environmental storytelling first — dialogue and cutscenes support it.' Must include at least one concrete technique for each narrative layer and explain how the layers connect so dedicated players feel rewarded, not just overwhelmed with extra text.

evals.prompt: Design a mentor character for my action RPG — a retired warrior who guides the player early on. Make them memorable rather than a generic quest-giver.
evals.expected_output: Must use the character bible structure with all 7 sections: Identity (name, age, appearance, role, faction), Motivation distinguishing public want vs hidden need (e.g., wants to train the player, needs to atone for a past failure), History (specific backstory events that explain their current retired state and training role), Personality (3-5 defining traits with speech patterns and quirks that make dialogue identifiable without names), Relationships (connections to other characters that create plot hooks), Arc (how they change or why they don't — a static mentor who never confronts their past is a wasted opportunity), Gameplay role (quest giver, trainer, companion, eventual boss?). Must use the Mentor archetype as a starting point and apply a subversion — the character bible table shows 'Mentor has hidden agenda' as the subversion example; must pick a specific subversion and justify it with history. Must avoid the 'walking exposition' anti-pattern — the mentor's personality must come through in actions, not monologues. Must address voice consistency so this character sounds distinct from other NPCs.

evals.prompt: I have 20 NPCs in my town but players say they all feel the same. How do I differentiate their voices, personalities, and roles so each one is memorable?
evals.expected_output: Must address the 'everyone talks the same' anti-pattern directly. Voice differentiation techniques: each NPC needs a distinct speech pattern (vocabulary level, sentence length, favorite phrases, topics they always redirect to), at least one defining quirk that appears in multiple interactions, and a clear motivation that drives what they talk about. Must cover the principle that 'characters want things' — every NPC needs a goal that makes their dialogue purposeful, not just flavor text. Covers 'characters exist beyond the player' — NPCs should reference events in their own lives, react to world state changes, have schedules or routines. Must address the 'personality-free quest giver' anti-pattern: NPCs who only deliver quests with no personality make the world feel like a menu system. Practical guidance: define a 3-word personality summary per NPC, write one 'signature line' that captures their voice, ensure no two NPCs share the same combination of role + personality type. Must include at least one technique for showing personality through action rather than dialogue.

evals.prompt: Design a skill tree for a mage class with fire, ice, and lightning branches. Show the database schema, how to enforce prerequisites server-side, and how to prevent circular dependencies.
evals.expected_output: Must use the Drizzle skill_nodes schema with: id (UUID), name, description, category ('active' | 'passive' | 'unlock' | 'prestige'), tier (integer for depth in tree), prerequisites (JSONB array of node IDs), cost (integer), effects (JSONB array with stat/modifier objects like { 'stat': 'fireDamage', 'modifier': '+15%' }), isVisible, metadata. Must use the player_skills schema with: playerId FK, nodeId FK, unlockedAt, snapshot JSONB (stores prerequisite state at unlock time for audit). Server-side unlock validation flow at POST /skills/unlock/:nodeId: fetch requested node, check all prerequisite nodeIds exist in player_skills for this player, check player has sufficient skill points, run Drizzle atomic transaction (deduct currency + insert player_skills row with snapshot). Cycle detection via DFS on the prerequisites adjacency list — must be enforced at admin write time, not at player unlock time. Must note the DAG constraint: cycles break validation, rollback, and respec logic.

evals.prompt: Players are complaining that respeccing their skill tree costs too much. What's the right respec mechanic design and how do I implement it?
evals.expected_output: Must apply the 'respec is player-friendly' principle: always provide a reset mechanism, options are free with cooldown or paid with soft currency, never lock players into permanent bad choices. Respec endpoint POST /skills/respec flow: check respec eligibility (cooldown check via timestamp comparison or currency balance check), run Drizzle atomic transaction that deletes all player_skills rows for the player, refunds skill points back to player's currency wallet (must refund exactly what was spent — use the snapshot JSONB on each player_skills row to reconstruct costs), invalidates the Redis cache of unlocked node IDs for that player session. Must cover cost scaling context: if using exponential cost curve (cost = baseCost * tier^exponent with exponent 1.5-2.0), late-game respec refunds are large — design the economy to absorb this. Must address progressive reveal: after respec, nodes that were previously unlocked and revealed become hidden again if their prerequisites are not met. Must note that effects are stored as JSONB data — respec only deletes player_skills rows, the skill_nodes definitions are unchanged.

evals.prompt: Set up player authentication with Discord OAuth for my Elysia game server. Players should stay logged in between sessions and I need to know their user ID on every game endpoint.
evals.expected_output: Must use BetterAuth with Drizzle adapter connected to Neon (PostgreSQL). Setup covers: installing better-auth, drizzle-orm, @neondatabase/serverless via bun add. Auth instance configuration with Discord OAuth provider (clientId, clientSecret from env), Drizzle adapter pointing to the database connection. Elysia integration: mount BetterAuth handler at /api/auth/*, add requireAuth middleware that calls auth.api.getSession({ headers }) and throws 401 if no session. Session tokens (not JWTs) for web games — must explain that sessions are stateful, server-controlled, and revocable, unlike JWTs. Discord OAuth flow covers: redirect to Discord, callback handling, user record creation in BetterAuth's managed tables. Must show how to derive user.id from the validated session in a game endpoint handler — never trust client-sent user IDs. Must run bunx drizzle-kit generate and bunx drizzle-kit migrate to create BetterAuth's required tables. Must cover CORS configuration requirement for OAuth redirects.

evals.prompt: I need three access levels in my game: regular players, moderators who can mute/ban players, and admins who can reset game state. How do I implement RBAC and protect my endpoints?
evals.expected_output: Must implement RBAC with a role enum: 'player' | 'moderator' | 'admin' with escalating privileges — never a single admin boolean. Role definitions with a permission matrix: player has game:play, moderator adds users:mute and users:ban, admin adds users:ban plus game:reset and all lower permissions. hasPermission(role, resource, action) utility function that checks the permission matrix. Elysia middleware patterns: requireAuth (validates session, attaches user to context), requireRole('moderator') (wraps requireAuth and additionally checks user.role), optionalAuth (attaches user if session exists, continues without error if not). Endpoint protection examples: game endpoints use .use(requireAuth), ban endpoint uses .use(requireRole('moderator')), reset endpoint uses .use(requireRole('admin')). Must fetch current role from the database on each request — never store roles in a cookie or client-side token, because role changes must take effect immediately without re-login. Must address the WebSocket auth pattern: validate session in beforeHandle before upgrading the connection. Rate limiting on auth endpoints: 5-10 attempts per minute on /auth/login and /auth/signup.

evals.prompt: Implement a gem bundle purchase flow where players buy 1000 gems for $9.99. Show the checkout session creation, webhook handling, and how to credit gems to the player account.
evals.expected_output: Must follow webhook-first fulfillment — never fulfill on checkout success redirect. Flow: (1) POST /store/checkout creates a Stripe Checkout session with playerId in metadata, priceId for the gem bundle, successUrl and cancelUrl. Session creation uses bun add stripe and the Stripe SDK server-side only. (2) Webhook handler at POST /webhooks/stripe: verify Stripe-Signature header using stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(body, sig, webhookSecret) before processing any event — unsigned webhooks can be spoofed. (3) Handle checkout.session.completed event: extract playerId from session.metadata, use idempotency key (session.id) to prevent double-grants on webhook replays. (4) Two-phase fulfillment: record the purchase in a purchases table first (status: 'pending'), then dispatch a BullMQ job to credit gems via currency table update. (5) On job completion, update purchase status to 'fulfilled'. Must use currency abstraction — credit gems (premium currency), never the real-money item directly. Must address the anti-pattern of storing Stripe keys in client code.

evals.prompt: A player disputed a charge and I need to handle the chargeback automatically — revoke their purchased gems and flag their account. How do I implement this?
evals.expected_output: Must listen for charge.dispute.created and charge.refunded Stripe webhook events in addition to the payment success events. Chargeback handler flow: (1) receive charge.dispute.created event, extract paymentIntentId or chargeId, look up the purchase record by Stripe payment reference. (2) Revoke rewards: deduct the granted gems from the player's currency balance (cannot go below zero — floor at zero and log the deficit). (3) Flag the player account: update a flagged boolean or add a 'chargeback' record to a player_flags table for manual review. (4) Use idempotency: check if the dispute has already been processed before revoking to prevent double-revocations on webhook replays. Must cover the two-phase fulfillment design: revocation mirrors fulfillment — update purchase status to 'revoked', dispatch a BullMQ revocation job that handles the currency deduction. Must address fraud velocity checks: if a player has multiple disputes, trigger an automatic account suspension rather than just flagging. Must note that unhandled disputes lead to Stripe account bans — always process dispute events.

evals.prompt: Add voice lines to my RPG's key NPCs using ElevenLabs. Show how to generate the audio, organize the files, and play them in sync with dialogue text.
evals.expected_output: Must use ElevenLabsClient with textToSpeech method, passing voiceId and model ('eleven_multilingual_v2' for multilingual support). Voice line generation: call client.textToSpeech(dialogueLine, { voiceId, model }) and write the returned audio buffer to a file path using Bun.write. File organization: public/audio/voice/{npcId}/{lineId}.mp3 pattern so each NPC's lines are grouped. Must cover voice consistency: assign one voiceId per NPC and document the mapping in a config file — do not change voice IDs between sessions or the NPC's voice will be inconsistent. Integration with dialogue system: audio state machine usage via audio.playVoice('npc_greeting') to play voice lines as one-shot audio on top of background music — the voice layer is independent from music and SFX layers. Must address mobile audio unlock: iOS and Android require user interaction before any audio plays; gate all audio including voice lines behind audio.unlockMobile(). Must note that ElevenLabs outputs MP3 or OGG directly with no conversion needed, unlike Lyria which outputs WAV. Per-layer volume controls: players need a separate voice volume slider independent of music and SFX.

evals.prompt: My game needs adaptive music that switches between exploration, combat, and boss themes without jarring cuts. How do I implement the audio state machine with crossfades?
evals.expected_output: Must implement an AudioManager using the audio state machine pattern with defined states: menu, exploration, combat, boss (and optionally social, idle). Each state maps to a track path, volume level, and crossfade duration in a config object (AUDIO_CONFIG using templates/sound-config.ts structure). State transitions via audio.transition('combat') — the manager crossfades from current track to new track using the configured duration, not a hard cut. Must cover the crossfade implementation: fade out current track volume over crossfadeDuration ms while fading in new track simultaneously. Preload strategy: preload combat and boss tracks during exploration so they are ready when needed — if combat starts and the track is still loading, the player hears silence (pitfall to avoid). Lyria music generation for tracks: use LyriaClient with prompt, negativePrompt ('no vocals, no sound effects, no silence'), and seed. Always log the seed in a manifest so tracks are reproducible. ffmpeg conversion: Lyria outputs 32.8s WAV at 48kHz stereo, convert to OGG (primary) and MP3 (fallback) using ffmpeg -i input.wav -c:a libvorbis -q:a 5 output.ogg. Must provide separate volume sliders for music, SFX, voice, and ambient layers.
